title: Session 2: AI for Research and Advanced Teaching
text:
Recap: The Compound Workflow (Emily)
~10 minutes
Last session: one prompt → one output. Today: chained steps where AI builds on its own prior work.

The cycle
Plan → Execute → Review → Clear context → Repeat
Why clear context? AI tools have limited memory. A fresh conversation with a clear task beats a long, muddled conversation. Document what you learned in a markdown file so you (and the AI) can pick up where you left off.


Key principles

Break big tasks into steps: Don’t ask “analyze my data and write a paper.” Ask “read my data dictionary and propose an analysis plan.”
Save intermediate outputs: Write results to files, not just chat.
Review each step: AI makes confident-sounding mistakes. Check before building on top.
Document as you go: Keep a running markdown file of decisions, prompts that worked, and lessons learned.

title: Session 2: AI for Research and Advanced Teaching
text:
Part 1: Research Pipeline Demo (Emily)
~35 minutes

Live demo: Raw data → analysis plan → code → report
Starting from a raw dataset, we’ll use Claude Code to:

Explore the data: “Read this CSV and describe what’s in it. How many observations? What are the variables?”
Propose an analysis plan: “Given these variables, suggest 3 analyses that would be interesting for a research paper on [topic].”
Write analysis code: “Write Stata code to run [analysis]. Use clear variable labels and save the output.”
Generate a summary report: “Based on these results, draft a 1-page summary with key findings, a table, and a limitations paragraph.”








NoteWhat this demo is NOT



This is not a replacement for careful research design. The AI doesn’t know your research question, institutional context, or identification strategy. It’s a tool for accelerating the mechanical parts — data cleaning, boilerplate code, first-draft summaries — so you can focus on the thinking.

~5 minutes

What to try next

Teaching: Use AI to draft a new assignment, create practice problems, or build a study guide
Research: Try the data exploration → analysis plan workflow on your own data
Writing: Use Claude Code to revise a draft, check references, or restructure a section



Ongoing resources

This site: eabeam.github.io/uvmecon-ai
Claude Code docs
UVM’s Microsoft Copilot (enterprise-protected, free)



The subscription question
AI subscriptions (Claude Pro, ChatGPT Plus) cost ~$20/month. UVM provides Microsoft Copilot for free. PDF funds may cover subscriptions — talk to your chair.

title: Getting Set Up
text:
For this bootcamp, the most useful distinction is not “browser vs. app.” It is:

Chat-based tools: good for asking questions, drafting text, analyzing uploaded files, and trying quick prompts.
Agentic tools: good for working on files, code, and multi-step tasks with direct access to your project or local environment.

Chat tools can still write code snippets or comment on code. What they usually do not do is directly search your folders, edit local files, or run commands on your machine. That is the role of the agentic tools we are focusing on for the terminal demos.

title: Getting Set Up
text:
For this bootcamp, the most useful distinction is not “browser vs. app.” It is:

Chat-based tools: good for asking questions, drafting text, analyzing uploaded files, and trying quick prompts.
Agentic tools: good for working on files, code, and multi-step tasks with direct access to your project or local environment.

Chat tools can still write code snippets or comment on code. What they usually do not do is directly search your folders, edit local files, or run commands on your machine. That is the role of the agentic tools we are focusing on for the terminal demos.

title: Getting Set Up
section: Option 1: Chat-based tools (easiest way to start)
text:
Option 1: Chat-based tools (easiest way to start)
These are point-and-click applications. Download, sign in, and start using them.

Claude (by Anthropic)
A conversational AI assistant. Available as a desktop app and in your browser.
Best for:

drafting and revising text
brainstorming
summarizing uploaded files
generating code snippets or examples

Not the same as Claude Code:

Claude in the browser or desktop app can help you talk through code and files
Claude Code can directly work in your terminal, read local project files, edit files, and run commands
Download: claude.ai/download (Mac and Windows)
Or use in browser: claude.ai
Subscription: Free tier available. Pro ($20/month) is the most straightforward paid option. Max starts at $100/month for substantially more usage.
Create an account: claude.ai — sign up with your email



ChatGPT (by OpenAI)
Another widely-used AI assistant with desktop and mobile apps.
Best for:

drafting and revising text
summarizing uploaded files
brainstorming
generating code snippets or examples

Not the same as Codex:

ChatGPT is primarily a chat interface
Codex is OpenAI’s coding agent, designed to work in your terminal, code editor, or Codex app with direct project actions
Download: openai.com/chatgpt/download (Mac and Windows)
Or use in browser: chatgpt.com
Subscription: Free tier available. Plus ($20/month) is the standard paid tier for most users.



UVM note: Microsoft Copilot
UVM provides enterprise-protected access to Microsoft Copilot Chat through your work account.

Access: go.uvm.edu/copilot
What it’s good for: low-friction web chat and some Microsoft 365 workflows
What it is not for: the terminal/file-access demos we are centering in this bootcamp
If you want deeper Office integration: ask about a Microsoft 365 Copilot license, which adds stronger Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Outlook integration
Why it’s not the main setup path here: for this bootcamp, we want tools that can clearly demonstrate the difference between chat-based help and agentic file/code workflows

title: Getting Set Up
text:
Option 2: Agentic tools (more powerful)
These run in your computer’s terminal — a text-based interface where you type commands. The terminal gives AI tools direct access to your files, which makes them much more powerful for real work.

What’s a terminal?
It’s an app that’s already on your computer:

Mac: Open Terminal (search for “Terminal” in Spotlight, or find it in Applications → Utilities)
Windows: Open PowerShell (search for “PowerShell” in the Start menu)

You type commands and press Enter. That’s it.


Installing Claude Code
Claude Code is an AI assistant that runs in your terminal. It can read your files, write documents, run code, and more.
This is the Anthropic tool that most clearly shows the difference between:

a chat tool that answers questions
an agentic tool that can act on a project

Step 1: Install Node.js (a tool that Claude Code needs to run)

Go to nodejs.org
Click the big green LTS download button
Run the installer — accept all the defaults

Step 2: Install Claude Code
Open your terminal and type this command, then press Enter:
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
Wait for it to finish (you’ll see some text scroll by — that’s normal).
Step 3: Launch it
Type this and press Enter:
claude
It will ask you to sign in. Follow the prompts.
How you pay for it:

Claude Pro ($20/month) or Claude Max (from $100/month) lets you sign in with your Claude account
Anthropic also supports logging in with an Anthropic Console account if you prefer API-based billing instead of a Claude subscription

For most faculty trying this on their own, Pro is the default place to start.


Installing OpenAI Codex
Codex is OpenAI’s coding agent, similar in spirit to Claude Code.
Step 1: Install Node.js (same as above — skip if you already did this)

Go to nodejs.org → download LTS → run installer

Step 2: Install Codex
npm install -g @openai/codex
Step 3: Launch it
codex
How you pay for it:

The simplest path is to sign in with an active ChatGPT subscription
You do not need a separate API account just to try the subscription-based Codex workflow

For most faculty, ChatGPT Plus is the simplest paid entry point if they want to try Codex.

Tool

Chat-based drafting, brainstorming, file uploads

Node.js + Claude login
Usually Claude Pro: $20/mo
Terminal workflow with file access and command execution


OpenAI Codex (agentic coding tool)
Node.js + ChatGPT login
Usually ChatGPT Plus: $20/mo
Terminal/code-editor/app workflow with project actions


Microsoft Copilot Chat
Included with UVM account
No extra cost
Enterprise-protected chat and light Office-adjacent support


Microsoft 365 Copilot
Ask about license availability
Extra institutional license
Deeper Office integration, not the main bootcamp workflow



For this bootcamp, the most relevant paid comparison is usually:

Claude Pro if you want to try Claude Code
ChatGPT Plus if you want to try Codex

PDF funds may cover subscriptions — ask your department chair.

title: Getting Set Up
text:
Resources
Some extra tools that aren’t required but are nice to have:

Ghostty (free) — A fast, modern terminal app. Looks nicer than the default Terminal/PowerShell. Purely cosmetic.
VS Code (free) — A code editor. Claude Code and GitHub Copilot both have VS Code extensions if you prefer a graphical interface over the terminal.


Markdown viewers / editors
AI tools often output rough-looking .md files. A good Markdown viewer or editor makes them render quickly as readable, attractive text.

Typora ($14.99) — Recommended default. It gives a live, clean preview and works well as a standalone app for opening Markdown files directly.
MarkText (free) — A simple open-source Markdown editor for macOS, Windows, and Linux.
Zettlr (free) — A stronger option for larger academic writing projects if you want more structure and writing features.
MacDown (free, macOS only) — A lightweight Mac-native Markdown editor worth considering.

If you just want one app that opens Markdown files and makes them look good immediately, Typora is the default recommendation here.
For broader reading beyond the bootcamp, see Outside Resources.

title: Session 1: Getting Started with AI Tools
text:
The AI Ladder
Where are you today?








Level
Description
Example




0
Haven’t tried it
—


1
Tried it once, wasn’t impressed
Asked ChatGPT a question, got a bad answer


2
Use it occasionally for simple tasks
Drafting emails, brainstorming


3
Regular user, integrated into workflow
Writing assistance, code help, literature search


4
Power user, multi-step workflows
Research pipelines, automated grading, custom tools


5
Building systems with AI
Custom agents, teaching tools, research infrastructure



Our goal today: Get everyone to at least Level 2, and show you what Levels 3–4 look like.






NoteFormat



This is a watch-along session — we’ll demo everything live. No installation required. Slides will be up during generation/loading time so you can follow the concepts while the AI works.

title: Session 1: Getting Started with AI Tools
text:
Part 1: Terminal + Claude Code (Emily)
~30 minutes

Why the terminal?
The desktop chat interfaces (ChatGPT, Claude.ai) are great for quick questions. But the terminal unlocks:

File access: AI can read and write files on your computer
Iteration: Work on a document across multiple steps without copy-pasting
Reproducibility: Save your workflow, rerun it later
Power tools: Claude Code can search your files, run code, and build things



What you’re seeing

Ghostty — a terminal app (any terminal works; this one just looks nice)
Claude Code — an AI assistant that runs in the terminal. Type claude to launch.
Live prompting — we type a request, the AI responds, we iterate.







TipThings you could try later on your own




“Read my syllabus at [path] and list all the due dates in a table”
“I have a CSV of student grades. Summarize the distribution and flag any outliers.”
“Draft a response to this student email: [paste email]”





Key concepts

Context window: The AI can only “see” what you show it. Be explicit about what you want.
Iteration: Your first output won’t be perfect. Refine: “Make it shorter,” “Add a section on X,” “Change the tone to be more formal.”
Markdown output: Claude Code writes in Markdown. Use Typora to view it nicely.



Live demo: terminal basics → Claude Code → iterating on output
